data.title: BlackRock Real Estate Securities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of -0.11% (Institutional shares) and -0.18% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, driven by positioning in the apartment, office, and data center sectors. The billboard, single tenant, and warehouse sectors hampered relative returns.
  • During the quarter, the fund had overweight positions in the data center, manufactured home, and apartment sectors, and underweight holdings in the billboard, single tenant, and industrials sectors.

★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM

Institutional shares rated against 210 Us Fund Real Estate Funds, as of 6/30/25, based on risk-adjusted total return. Ratings are determined monthly and subject to change. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics.††

Portfolio management

Rajan Rehan, Curtis Yee

4.34
Essex Property Trust (ESS) 3.27

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% in equity investments engaged primarily in real estate industries inside the United States and up to 20% outside the United States (deriving at least 50% of revenues or profits from commercial, industrial or residential real estate industries or with 50% of its assets in real estate interests).

Contributors

Detractors

At a security level, overweight positions in Iron Mountain (+31 basis points), Hudson Pacific Properties (+30bps), and SmartStop Self Storage REIT (+24bps) contributed. At a sector level, overweight holdings in apartments (+38bps), offices (+23bps), and data centers were beneficial.

At a security level, overweight positions in Iron Mountain (+31 basis points), Hudson Pacific Properties (+30bps), and SmartStop Self Storage REIT (+24bps) contributed. At a sector level, overweight holdings in apartments (+38bps), offices (+23bps), and data centers were beneficial.

Further insight

Despite trade policy uncertainty, long-term real estate sector fundamentals remain positive. Supply constraints are driving pricing power, with elevated interest rates impacting supply deliveries and benefiting real estate investment trust pricing power. Listed real estate valuations are attractive and inexpensive relative to broader equities. Stimulus measures across Europe and Asia could act as key catalysts and bolster regional economic growth. Secular trends are accelerating, with increased protectionism likely to speed up development in subsectors such as logistics and digital real estate.

For Investor A shares: Total 1.25%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 1.00%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 07/02/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.75%; for Investor A shares: 1.00%.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Stock values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Investing in small- and mid-cap companies may entail greater risk than large-cap companies, due to shorter operating histories, less seasoned management or lower trading volumes. Non-diversification of investments means that more assets are potentially invested in fewer securities than if investments were diversified, so risk is increased because each investment has a greater effect on performance. Concentrating investments in single sector means that performance will be more susceptible to factors affecting that sector and more volatile than funds that invest in many different sectors. If interest rates rise, the value of convertible securities usually falls. Convertible securities are subject to the risk that the issuer will not pay interest or dividends and their market value may change based on their credit rating or the perception of their creditworthiness. A convertible security also is subject to the same risks as common stock. The main risk of real estate securities is that their value may go down depending on economies, vacancy rates, interest rates, tenant bankruptcies, amount of new construction in an area, laws and regulations and real estate maintenance and improvement. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. Many real estate-related securities issuers are highly leveraged, increasing the risk of such securities.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of June 30, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Global Real Estate Index series is designed to represent general trends in eligible real estate equities worldwide. 2 The information shows you how the Fund’s performance has varied for the periods since inception and provides some indication of the risks of investing in the Fund. The table compares the Fund’s performance to that of the FTSE EPRA Nareit United States Index. Effective June 1, 2020, the Fund changed its benchmark to the MSCI US REIT Index, as BlackRock considers this benchmark to be more representative of the sectors in which the Fund invests.†† The Morningstar RatingTM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product s monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund was rated against the following numbers of U.S.-domiciled Us Fund Real Estate funds over the following time periods:210 in the last 3 years, 193 in the last 5 years and 149 in the last 10 years. With respect to these Us Fund Real Estate funds, the fund received a Morningstar Rating of 3, 3 and 4 stars for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Other classes may have different performance characteristics.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

data.title: GTERU: A Good Place To Park Some Of Your "Safe" Money
data.description:
Because of the recent market euphoria, investors are finding good opportunities to sell fully priced or overpriced investments. But one problem that can occur is an excessive cash build-up. And it is getting harder to find good places to earn decent returns without taking on significant risk.

In this article, I discuss a good way to invest some of your "safe" bucket in pre-merger SPACs.

There are many SPACs available to use for the strategy. For this article, I have chosen Global Terra Acquisition Corp.. (NASDAQ:GTERU).

SPACs are structured to offer little or no downside risk as long as you sell before the business combination is actually completed. There can be good upside "optionality" from the more successful deals. In most cases, the units also contain extra warrants or rights (or sometimes both).

The basic idea of the strategy is that you can buy GTERU units and then split them up into its components and receive common (GTERA), warrants (GTERW) and rights (GTERR).

If the management team can't find a business to acquire in the allowed time period, the original SPAC unit investors can get their money back by cashing in the GTERA common for the original $10 trust value plus accrued interest earned from Treasury Bills owned by the Trust.

Here is some of the key information you need to know for GTERU:

IPO Date: July 9, 2025

Gross Proceeds: $152 Million

Initial Trust Value Per Share: $10.00

Unit Composition: Each unit (GTERU) contains 1 share of common (GTERA), ¾ of a warrant (GTERW) to purchase the common at $11.50 per share and one right (GTERR) which entitles the holder to purchase 1/10 of a common share.

Business Target: The Company may pursue an acquisition in any business sector, but it plans to focus on companies in the food, water and agribusiness sectors. This is the relevant section from the SPAC prospectus which describes their ideal business target:

"We may pursue an initial business combination in any business or industry. However, we intend to focus our search on target businesses within the agribusiness and water sectors, primarily in food-tech, ag-tech, bio-tech, controlled environment agriculture and open field crops in the case of agribusiness, and in water utility, water treatment, pipelines, desalination and other water solutions within the water sectors. Our geographic focus will be the Americas, with particular emphasis on North America, including Canada, the United States, and Mexico, where we believe the expertise of our management team and Advisors will provide us with a competitive advantage in completing a successful initial business combination."

Underwriter: D. Boral Capital. This is a small profitable investment bank with a strong balance sheet. But they are not considered top-tier SPAC underwriters. This likely explains why the terms offered on this SPAC (both rights and warrants offered) are more generous than for most other SPACs.

SPAC Management Team: The management team has extensive experience in the agribusiness and water sectors along with a background in M&A and raising capital.

They have participated in the IPOs of three prior SPAC deals with mixed results:

  1. Bite Acquisition Corp. which completed a business combination with (ABVE). ABVE currently trades for $1.88, so while the sponsors were successful in completing a deal, the following post-merger performance has been poor. Of course, investors who cashed out of the trust BEFORE the business combination did just fine and received the trust value.

  2. Digital World Acquisition Corp. which completed a business combination with TMTG which has now re-branded as (DJT). DJT currently trades for $18.12, and is one of the few "de-spacs" that are still trading well above the original $10 SPAC unit price.

  3. Agrinam Acquisition Corp which traded on the Toronto Stock Exchange. Agrinam did not complete a business combination and has appealed for an extension from the Toronto exchange.

Since the SPAC management team appears to have a relationship with the Trump family, it is possible that the rights and warrants for GTER may have some future "optionality" value.

Unit Split Date: The units for GTERU were available to be split after July 25, 2025. At this point, splitting the SPAC units is optional and the units are still trading along with SPAC common(GTERA), SPAC warrants(GTERW) and SPAC rights (GTERR).

Here are the closing prices as of August 26, 2025:

GTERU: $10.17 GTERA: $9.955 GTERW: $0.1299 GTERR: $0.13

If you add up the components of the GTERU unit you get :

9.955 + (0.75)(0.1299) +0.13= $10.1824

Note: This is slightly higher than the last unit price. But there is no clear arbitrage because of bid-asked spreads.

Estimated Liquidation Redemption Date: October 10, 2026

Note: Many SPAC deals are consummated before the completion deadline date. It is important to monitor the progress of the SPAC management team, since shareholder votes can occur before that date. You will usually see a press release announcing a letter of intent or definitive merger agreement well before the shareholder vote. This gives you time to exit the SPAC common shares well before the shareholder vote while you still have the protection of the $10+ trust value floor.

I usually trade SPACs at Fidelity which has a good Corporate Actions page that alerts you to potential SPAC tenders well in advance.

When Do You Exit SPAC Investments?

There are many good ways to exit a SPAC unit trade, depending on your risk profile and how much time you have for more active trading.

  • If GTERU appreciates significantly in price, you can simply sell the unit even before you split them. In some cases with hot SPAC IPOs, you can sometimes earn more than 10% in a short period of time. There may be other situations when the SPAC management team announces a proposed business combination that the market likes and the unit price may shoot up for a brief period.

  • Another strategy is to simply split up the units and then trade out of the components after they settle in your account. There may be wide bid-asked spreads, but often large trading inefficiencies, so it is worthwhile to check out the math and sell the unit components if they trade at a premium to the units price.

  • For an investor who likes the SPAC management team and is willing to take some more risk, you can often sell only the GTERA common shares to recapture your original investment, and keep the GTERW warrants or GTERR rights for a longer term trade.

  • When this occurs, you are essentially "playing with house money". Keep in mind that the warrants in particular are very high risk. But that may be OK, if you have already recouped your initial investment.

  • It is very important to exit the SPAC common (GTERA) before the shareholder vote on the proposed business combination. Once the business combination is approved, you lose the protection of the $10+ trust floor price.

  • Investors who are risk averse or yield oriented may want to sell off the rights and warrants well before the business combination is completed.

How do you split up SPAC units?

SPAC units can usually be split within a few months after the filing of the IPO prospectus. To get the exact terms for any specific IPO, you need to read the prospectus. But the company usually releases a press release which confirms when the unit split possibility is effective.

When you want to split up a SPAC unit into its components, you need to call your broker's Corporate Actions department to split them up. The process of splitting the units usually takes several business days, depending on the broker.

Broker Fees: The cost to split up a SPAC unit into its components can range from zero to as much as a few hundred dollars. I usually trade SPACs at Fidelity where you can do SPAC unit splits for free by calling their Corporate Actions team. I believe that at E-Trade it is also free to split SPACs if your account is large enough, but I am not certain about this.

For smaller size positions, it is not always necessary to split up the units and it is sometimes easier to just sell them whole. But the units can be much less liquid than the SPAC common. So for a much larger position, it is usually much easier to first split up the units, then exit by selling off the components.

A Brief Overview of SPAC Investing

I thought I would include a brief overview of SPAC investing updated from one of my previous SPAC articles from a few years ago:

Here are the basic stages in the life of a SPAC:

  • 1) The SPAC raises money in an IPO.

  • 2) The proceeds from the IPO are invested in a Trust Account that invests in short-term US Treasuries.

  • 3) The SPAC management team has a defined time period to find and merge with a Private Company that takes the company public.

  • 4) If they fail to find a Private Company to acquire within a defined time period (usually around 24 months), the SPAC must return the Trust assets to investors with interest. But in some cases, the sponsors add additional money to the Trust in return for an additional time extension.

  • 5) When the SPAC management finds a deal, shareholders vote whether or not to approve it.

Source: The Harvard Law School Forum on Corporate Governance | The leading online blog in the fields of corporate governance and financial regulation.

  • In many ways, SPACs are like zero coupon bonds, with the underlying return of short-term US treasuries plus the upside of a successful acquisition. But this is only true if you exit your position before the business combination is completed.

  • Once the business combination is completed, post-SPAC investments can be very risky and historically have had very poor returns.

  • Unlike bonds, the excess return from early SPAC investing does not come from adding credit risk or duration risk. But excess returns can be generated if you can identify SPAC management teams that can accomplish a successful merger with a company that the "market" finds attractive.

  • In horse track betting, there is a strategy called "betting on the jockey". Investing in a SPAC IPO or buying SPAC units after the IPO can be quite similar. But instead of a single jockey, you are betting on a SPAC management team that is also responsible for selecting which horse they will ride on. But a conservative investor would exit on the "price pop" before the business combination is actually completed.

What to Look For When Selecting a SPAC Unit to Buy

1) SPAC Leadership Team: As a general rule, the better SPAC teams offer less warrants in their IPOs. It is also a plus, if the management team has already done several successful SPAC deals before this one.

2) SPAC Focus: As a general rule, you would want to target "popular" areas that the market likes, such as AI or FinTech. It is better to avoid out-of-favor areas, unless there is a big price discount.

3) Warrant Terms: If a SPAC offers a full warrant per unit, it is usually a lower quality team. The better management teams usually offer 1/4 warrant, 1/3 warrant or 1/2 warrant per unit, and those are good ones to focus on. GTERU offered ¾ warrant plus a right since the management team is not considered to be of the highest quality.

4) Enhanced Trust Account: Some of the lower quality management teams offer an enhanced initial trust value above $10.00. This increases the "yield" on the SPAC units, especially when you can purchase the units or common below $10.00.

A SPAC Boom May Be Re-Igniting

In 2021, there was a SPAC IPO boom with hundreds of issues coming out. But then in 2022 through 2024, the number of SPAC IPOs dropped way off. But now in 2025, there has been a big resurgence in SPAC IPOs again with Cantor as the leading underwriter. We are still not near 2021 levels, but this may still be the early stages of another SPAC boom.

Risk Discussion

  • The safest way to invest in GTERU is to immediately split up the units, sell off the rights and warrants and just keep the GTERA common. Of course, you can also just buy the GTERA common for around $9.96 which is about 10 cents below my current estimated value for the GTER Trust account. You would then set up a situation with no downside risk as long as you can wait until the SPAC completion date with no deal, or a business combination is announced.

  • But there is one big caveat - you must exit the SPAC common (GTERA) before the shareholder vote on the proposed business combination. Once the business combination is approved, you lose the protection of the trust floor, and there can be considerable downside risk. After this stage, it is like any other small cap equity investment.

Concluding Remark

At the current price levels, the GTERA common price of $9.96 is well below the estimated $10.05 trust value. If you purchase it now, there is effectively no downside risk and basically a T-Bills plus 1% return between now and the date of the business combination in the worst case scenario. The units at $10.17 have slightly more risk, because there are embedded warrants and rights. But you can split the units any time with only a few days delay, and there is more upside "optionality" because of the rights and warrants.

By constructing a portfolio of SPAC issues that trade at or below the trust value, you essentially have no long-term downside risk, but you still retain the possibility of some upside if any of these issues ever "pop-up" in price. You do have to be careful to keep track of the SPAC completion dates and any business combinations that are proposed.

By using this approach, you can create a "Heads you can win a little, Tails you can win more" situation. Pre-merger SPACs can be a good place to park some extra cash in the current market environment. There are several ETFs and closed-end funds that purchase pre-merger SPACs with some or all of their portfolio, but they generally charge around 1% a year as a management fee. For more active investors who have the time required, you can execute the strategy yourself and save the fees.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NNN REIT: Dividend Aristocrat On Sale
data.description:
Warren Buffett at one time remarked that his portfolio isn’t comprised of fast movers, but rather of ones that move at a glacial pace. Perhaps there is some wisdom to that, as slow-moving dividend stocks give investors plenty of time to accumulate before they are favored by the market again. Top of mind names include Altria (MO) and Exxon Mobil (XOM), both of which have traded at bargain basement prices within the past 5 years, letting the magic of compounding reinvested dividends do their work before their prices moved significantly up.

This brings me to NNN REIT, Inc. (NYSE:NNN), which I last covered in July, highlighting its highly diversified portfolio, robust acquisitions and progress on re-leasing properties. NNN has been rangebound over the past 9 months and currently sits just 0.8% higher than my last visit. As shown below, NNN sports a 12x forward P/FFO and a 5.7% yield.

In this article, I revisit NNN and include recent business results, and discuss why income and value investors ought to focus on the stock while it remains undervalued. So, let’s get started!

Why NNN?

NNN REIT is one of the original publicly-traded net lease REITs, alongside peer Realty Income Corp. (O). It currently has a portfolio of 3,663 properties that are diversified across 400+ tenants and 35+ lines of trade. Most of its properties are in e-commerce and recession resistant categories. As shown below, automotive services, convenience stores, and fast food restaurants being the top 3 categories representing 43% of total base rent.

NNN recently demonstrated steady results, with FFO per share growing by 1.2% YOY to $0.84. It also continues to see strong profitability with an NOI margin of 98%. Lease renewals were strong, with 17 out of 20 leases being renewed at rental rates 108% above the prior rents. Additionally, NNN successfully leased 7 properties to new tenants at 105% above prior rents.

NNN also made robust acquisitions, in which it invested $230 million across 45 properties. They came with an attractive cap rate of 7.4% and a long average lease term that exceeds 17 years. This contributed to $460 million in total acquisitions in the first half of the year across 127 properties.

At the same time, management is actively pruning non-core and vacant properties, by selling 23 properties for $50 million in Q2 and 33 properties year-to-date for $65 million. The income-producing properties were sold at a cap rates equal to 170 bps below acquisition yields, which means that NNN got price appreciation and income over the lifespan of ownership. Importantly, NNN’s occupancy remains solid at 98%, sitting just a tick below its 20-year average occupancy of 98.2%.

Looking ahead, management raised the midpoint of its full year adjusted funds from operations, or AFFO, per share guidance to $3.43, representing 2.4% growth from the prior year. Management also increased the acquisition outlook to the $650 million range. Necessity-based and service-oriented tenants remain the core focus for NNN, as these categories make up 85% of annual base rents. These categories have limited direct exposure to tariffs. In particular, management is seeing robust activity in the auto services sector. This is driven by the higher cost of new vehicle prices due to inflation and tariffs.

According to S&P Global the average U.S. vehicle age rose from 12.6 years in 2024 to 12.8 years in 2025. This bodes well for the auto services segment as Americans on average keep their cars for longer and increasingly opt for repairs in lieu of new vehicle purchases.

Importantly, NNN carries a strong BBB+ credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a strong balance sheet with a solid fixed-charge coverage ratio of 4.2x. NNN also has well-staggered debt maturities, making it less susceptible to interest rate swings, as shown below.

The strong balance sheet and steady rental income supports NNN’s appealing 5.7% dividend yield. The dividend is well-covered by a 69% payout ratio. NNN is also a dividend aristocrat with 34 consecutive years of dividend growth and a 5-year dividend CAGR of 2.5%.

Lastly, I continue to see value in NNN at the current price of $42.39 with a forward P/FFO of 12.5, sitting fare below its historical P/FFO of 16.1, as shown below.

While NNN’s low single digit FFO/share growth rate isn’t particularly expensive, that is a function of recent tenant troubles, management has dealt with through property re-leasing. Given the overall health of the portfolio and robust acquisition pipeline, I believe NNN could return to a 4-5% FFO/share growth rate annually. This, combined with the 5.7% yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation over time could result in at least market-level total returns as a base scenario and potentially market-beating total returns. This comes with a far higher yield than that of the S&P 500 (SP500, SPY).

Risks to NNN include the recent bankruptcy filing case of NNN’s tenant, At Home. While NNN owns 11 At Home properties, none were on the initial closure list and rent payments remain current. Moreover, higher interest rates could raise NNN’s cost of debt funding and slow its pace of acquisitions. Higher cost of debt could also eat into rent growth.

Investor Takeaway

NNN REIT offers investors a compelling mix of stability, income, and value. It trades well below its historical valuation at just 12.5x forward P/FFO with a secure 5.7% dividend yield. Management continues to execute effectively, boosting AFFO guidance, recycling capital efficiently, and expanding its necessity-focused portfolio with high-yielding acquisitions. With a rock-solid balance sheet and well-covered yield, NNN presents an attractive opportunity for long-term income and value investors willing to be patient before the market revalues this steady performer.

data.title: IBND: Developed Markets Bonds For A Falling Dollar
data.description:
Thesis

A weak dollar in 2025 has been the topic of many discussions, with investors seeing benefits in diversifying into developed and EM markets equities and bonds. We have covered two local currency EM bond funds where we can see that benefit, with today's article covering an investment grade fixed income fund from developed markets, namely the SPDR Bloomberg International Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:IBND).

As per its objective, the fund:

Seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the price and yield performance of the Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex-USD > $1B: Corporate Bond Index. Seeks to provide a broad exposure to the global investment grade, fixed rate, fixed income corporate markets outside the United States

We are going to have a closer look at IBND and its analytics as well as its forward in today's macro context.

Fund holdings

The name holds investment grade corporate paper from various developed markets:

  • United States: 27.4%
  • France: 20.7%
  • Germany: 10.7%
  • UK: 10%
  • Spain: 6%
  • Italy: 5.6%
  • Netherlands: 4.8%
  • Switzerland: 2.9%
  • Belgium: 2.2%
  • Japan: 2.1%
  • Sweden: 1.7%
  • Denmark: 1.5%
  • Canada: 1%
  • Australia: 1%

Although the top exposure, the U.S. only represents 27% of the holdings. The quality of the names is all investment grade:

  • AAA: 1.5%
  • AA: 11.2%
  • A: 56.3%
  • BBB: 31%

Please note the name is overweight single-A names rather than the more risky BBB corporates. Most of the holdings fall in the financials sector which represents 55% of the holdings, followed by industrials at 40%. There are over 900 names in the portfolio, thus a very granular fund.

Risk analytics

Let us look at some risk analytics now for this name:

  • AUM: $0.44 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.5%
  • Duration: 4.6 years
  • Portfolio YTM: 3.14%
  • OAS: 75 bps
  • SEC yield: 2.6%

The ETF is a bit on the expensive side at a 0.5% expense ratio. We usually see figures here below 0.1% for large manager funds like this one. The duration is intermediate at 4.6 years, while the yields are very low, but characteristic of investment grade paper.

Unhedged profile

The main attraction for this name should be the unhedged profile. The ETF holds many local currency bonds that are not hedged via FX forwards or swaps. This means that dollar weakness translates into gains for IBND via a larger NAV. And the dollar has been weak in 2025:

The ICE US Dollar Index (DXY) is down -9.4% this year, with the market pricing in lower Fed Funds as well as a ballooning deficit. This dynamic has helped the name outperform its U.S. centric peers.

Performance - the dollar has provided a boost

While U.S. centric peers have been helped by tighter corporate spreads only, IBND has been boosted by a weaker dollar:

When compared to the iShares 5-10 Yr Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGIB) and the iShares iBoxx Inv Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), IBND has done very well. Please remember IGIB has a duration of 6 years, while LQD runs a 8 years duration.

An investor in IBND should not chase yield because it is very small. An investor here takes a view on the dollar and on the local rates in each country that has bonds in this portfolio. As local rates move lower, IBND is set to gain. Similarly a weak dollar will boost IBND.

Who should be interested in IBND?

The main theme for this ETF is a weak dollar. Investors who just look at yield will be disappointed with the 2.6% 30-day SEC yield. Yet the fund is up over +14% this year! The main driver behind this astounding performance has been a weak dollar and lower European Central Bank ('ECB') rates in 2025.

If we look at the ETF's portfolio we will notice tight spreads as well, similar to what we have in the U.S. Thus going forward the name should appeal to investors looking to diversify away from dollar only holdings and investors looking to profit from a weaker dollar.

Some folks like to play weaker dollar via ETFs targeting certain exchange rates, but IBND is a better solution since it holds a large portfolio of currencies, not only specific pairs. Moreover the name does generate a yield (albeit small) thus being a positive carry name. Is IBND exciting when the dollar does not weaken? No. Does it provide for a good portfolio diversifier in today's environment? We think so.

Conclusion

IBND is a fixed income exchange traded fund. The name holds a portfolio of investment grade corporate bonds from developed markets and has an intermediate duration of roughly 5 years. The name is not hedged, meaning that if the U.S. dollar continues to weaken, the fund's NAV will be positively impacted, and investors can expect gains from currency appreciation in addition to bond performance. The fund's dividend yield is not inspiring at 2.6%, and a long position is that this ETF is a play on the dollar and local rates as well as a portfolio diversifier. We like IBND better than local currencies equities which are more volatile. Given the start of a dollar macro cycle we are a 'Buy' for this name with a 16-month time horizon in mind.

data.title: Blackstone: Key Beneficiary Of Lower Rates
data.description:
Introduction

Blackstone (NYSE:BX) is one of the top picks in the private markets asset manager space. I wrote about the stock before and issued a buy rating at the time. Since my initial coverage the stock has returned 32%, far in excess of the broader market. Blackstone is by its nature an interest rate sensitive stock. The private markets business relies on transaction activity and deal making enthusiasm.

Interest rates, the cost of financing and ultimately the discount rate for valuations are key determinants in deal making and the realized valuations of said deals. Markets are currently pricing a meaningful decline in rates over the next 12 months, looking out to July 2026 we see a full percentage point of cuts currently priced in. The added pressure on the Fed from the presidency while questionable in some regards, it stands to directly benefit the likes of Blackstone if rates come down at a quicker pace than they otherwise would.

The potential kicker for the stock and indeed its peers is the news of an executive order which seeks to open up the vast 401k pool of capital to investment in private markets among other things. All told I think the outlook for the stock remains very positive, maybe even more so than when I wrote about it last time. I am reiterating a buy rating on Blackstone, the key drivers remain in place and a potentially large new one has emerged since my last coverage.

Executive Order

In August, President Trump signed an executive order directing regulators to expand access to private market assets in 401k plans. While no immediate impact will be felt, the Department of Labor are set to review, so the direction of travel looks to be clear. The democratization of private access continues to grow. Traditionally the arena of large institutional capital such as endowments, private markets have been increasingly become available to private wealthy individuals in recent years through open-ended semi-liquid vehicles. Blackstone has been a leader in the space with vehicles such as Blackstone Private Equity or BXPE and Blackstone Real Estate or BREIT offering private individuals access to leading private markets solutions.

While these vehicles do lead to a higher fee load than public alternatives and a reduction in liquidity, BREIT for instance has vastly outperformed the public REIT index, justifying the trade-offs. A 401k private market solution would likely need to be an evergreen type solution such as a BREIT as opposed to the traditional closed end buyout fund which draws down capital over a number of years and winds up after a set number of years. Given the long duration nature of pension assets, a lack of full liquidity is not an issue so there is no problem in that respect.

Interest Rates

Deal making is by its nature an interest rate sensitive affair. Market valuations tend on average to be lower when rates are higher as the discount rate is higher. Conversely valuations tend to bounce when rates are falling and the cost of capital is becoming cheaper. Blackstone is sitting on plenty of companies it purchases when rates were higher and valuation depressed. The success of the business comes in the ability to improve those companies purchased and sell them at a higher price than they were originally bought for. Interest rates falling makes that process much easier.

Current market pricing is for rates to be a full percentage point lower by this time next year. This if it comes to past, ought to provide a nice tailwind for valuations. The topic of Fed rate setting has been getting plenty of coverage lately given the attacks on the Fed by the president. Ultimately if this leads to lower rates then Blackstone stands to benefit. The firm doesn't necessarily need rates to fall -- it can still make perfectly fine profits in a steady rate environment. However, declining rates would help them with realizations and ultimately performance based revenue.

Valuation

The valuation for Blackstone tends to jump around a bit as the stock goes through periods of bumper performance based fees and periods of relative quiet. The P/E for the next 12 months looks expensive vs history but on a PEG basis the stock looks cheap. The reason for this is the PEG is incorporating earnings over a number of years and thus is baking in the expected rebound in deal making activity and thus P&L. With this in mind I think Blackstone looks fine from a valuation perspective. The key question of course is how long it takes for the performance earnings to really get going, current market forecasts of course could be wrong. Much will depend on the path of interest rates and their ultimate impact on valuations.

Risks

The big salient risk to my thesis is persistently higher interest rates. Rates have come down somewhat from their peak but remain relatively high. The market is pricing in an expectation for rates to decline from here and that would be a tailwind for Blackstone. If rate declines were to stall that would stall the momentum behind the stock. Worse still, if the Fed were forced to pivot and start to raise rates that would undoubtedly dent Blackstone in the short term.

I think the Fed have erred on the side of caution as it pertains to rate cutting, which gives me the sense that rate rises from here are unlikely but we could see the pace of rate cutting happen faster than current market pricing. Whatever the direction of interest rates in the near term I still feel positive about the long term trajectory for the business. Blackstone stands to benefit from increased retail and private wealth participation in private markets. The president's executive order to open up 401k plans to private assets can potentially unlock a huge pool of capital for leading private market places like Blackstone.

The potentially bigger risk for the stock is a systemic event in private markets that badly dents investor confidence. The banking industry suffered greatly in the great financial crisis and as a result banks have been out of favor with investors for a very long time. We can see this in names like Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) which are only now starting to approach their pre-GFC valuations following more than a decade of frightful investors staying clear.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my buy rating on Blackstone today. The stock has done great since I first issued a buy rating and I think the current interest rate outlook stands to be positive for the stock. Private markets as an asset class still have huge room to run in terms of asset allocation in areas such as private wealth. Before now, large pools of assets in the 401k pension channel have been prohibited from investing in private markets but that looks set to change. This is a huge new pool of capital for Blackstone to tap into.

The pension channel will likely be best suited to open-ended or evergreen type investment solutions, a vertical in which Blackstone has strong product in the market such as BXPE, BREIT and BCRED. The stock is not without risks but I think this is a secular story of increased allocations to private markets over time and Blackstone as the biggest private markets player is well positioned to avail of the opportunity. I think the road might be a bit bumpy but the destination looks good, staying buy on Blackstone.

data.title: NQP: Useful For PA Residents But Care Is Needed
data.description:
Mechanics matter

The nuts and bolts of how things are constructed, how they work, are important. This becomes more so the more complex the machinery is. Thus it's a darn good idea to understand how something is working internally before we decide whether this is something we want to use, something likely to achieve a goal in line with our own desires.

This is as true of financial instruments as it is of any other piece of complex machinery. Therefore we need an explanation of those moving parts before we can decide whether we're interested. Or not, as the case may be.

Nuveen Pennsylvania Quality Municipal Income Fund (NYSE:NQP)

The most obvious thing we should say at the start here is that NQP is aimed at taxpaying residents of Pennsylvania. If you're not one of those then this instrument just isn't going to be of great interest. So, unless you're really enamoured of my prose, if you're not paying tax in PA now would be a good time to do something else other than read this.

This is a municipal bond fund. Which in itself requires a little explanation.

There are, essentially, three types of bond. Treasuries are lending to the Federal Government and as Uncle Sam hasn't gone bust this past two centuries (although, as other countries have proven, this is no certainty for the future) we accept to assume that there is no credit risk here. It's not wholly and exactly true but it's a basic underpinning of how our financial markets work that we act like it is.

There is still though interest rate risk. A bond issued yesterday, when interest rates were 3%, is worth a different amount from one issued today, when interest rates are 4%. That difference in capital value is whatever the net present value of $3 or $4 a year on each $100 of holding is. Clearly, that depends upon how long the bond lasts for (the "tenor") for a 30 year bond will change in value more than a 30 day one.

It's also true that coupons - the interest rate paid - on bonds rise the longer that tenor of the bond. So we've a little difficulty here for of course we'd like to maximise our income. But that means having to take on more interest rate risk. There is no cute way out of this either. We simply do have to carry that extra interest rate risk if we want to gain the higher coupon.

The second grouping is corporates, lending to companies. They can - as we all know - go bust so this introduces credit risk. Therefore corporate bonds should pay more than Treasuries and they do.

There is a way around higher credit risk. It's not a complete solution but it aids, and that's diversification. Hold little bits of many different issues/issuers and if one goes bust that's an annoyance not a disaster. For most bonds - all three types - we can usually buy $1,000 lots at issue and trade them in at redemption. But the secondary markets are a little different. They can be thin, illiquid, and also in rather larger sizes. The European corporate bond markets trade in $200k par value minimum lot sizes for example.

The way around this is to buy into a bond fund. The fund is large enough to be diversified even in those secondary markets, we own a fraction of the fund.

The third type of bond is municipals. Lendng to subnational governmental bodies - states to the local water board. These can go bust - the default rate is low, perhaps 0.1% by one estimate but it exists - so munis should pay more than Treasuries even if maybe less than corporates. That's not how it works out - munis pay less than Uncle Sam.

The trick is that municipals' income is Federal income tax free and, usually, a within state bond is state income tax free for within state income tax payers.

That's how we get bond funds holding municipals which are aimed at the residents of a particular state, as here with NQP, aimed at residents of Pennsylvania.

But how good is a muni fund?

Because the value (OK, the added value) of a muni fund is in the tax break the, erm, valuation of that extra added value depends upon the tax rate that would be charged upon a taxable income. This will vary for different taxpayers as tax rates do vary dependent upon income bands and so on. So, what we do is convert to a "tax equivalent yield". What would a taxable investment have to pay as a yield to equal the post tax cash in hand we're getting as a result of the tax break? Fortunately people work out tables of this for us:

There's no exact number at which munis become "the right" decision. Some people really hate paying taxes and of course there are those who so love government that they'll happily pay more. Well, there are those who tell us so that is. But the usual rule of thumb is that if your marginal income tax rate - the rate on your next $ of income - is 24% or above then you probably will benefit from munis as opposed to taxable yields.

So, if you're currently making $50k or more and are resident in PA then NQP could well be of interest to you. Well, assuming that you're saving, you want income from your savings and so on.

Fund details

NQP is a closed end fund. This means that our money, when we buy, does not go into the fund. Rather, we buy off a selling stockholder. One implication of this is that the fund cannot suffer a run, a redemption cascade - there's a certain safety to that. Although, given how liquid municipal bonds are that's not hugely a concern anyway. Another point is that a closed fund can be leveraged and NQP is. 42.95%. If the coupon on the bonds held is higher than the short term borrowing rate then this will juice returns.

The third implication is that a closed end fund can move well off net asset value.

A usual solution to this is that the managers will boost the yield by increasing the distribution to include some "return of capital". This should bring the net asset value down closer to market price. Of course, it's also possible that this doesn't, quite, happen and all that does is that the discount to NAV stays constant and the market price drops. Or even a combination of both effects.

We rather have a choice as to what blend of those two stories we wish to apply here.

More details

The top holdings are:

We can see the full list here. There are 294 holdings so they are actively managing exposure, this is not the entire universe - as an index fund would typically hold - of available bonds. Fees are 0.66% a year which is also an indication of active management - an index fund would be a little over one tenth of that.

Credit ratings of holdings:

And maturities:

We can see what the investment policy is from those two charts. Go long tenor in order to maximise coupon and income but at the same time be selective on credit quality so as to reduce credit risk. So, little credit risk, rather more interest rate risk. This is neither good nor bad it's simply a choice.

So, how well does NQP do?

The headline yield there is 8.67%. Which is really pretty darn good - so good it's off the chart of those calculations of tax-e yield. But we should also take that with more than a little salt. For as we've also seen when the NAV discount blows out a fund can decide to boost the reported yield by return of capital. This, in theory, lowers that discount. It's also possible that there are less informed investors who simply buy on the yield and don't realise how much of their supposed income is really just that RoC. If that were true then the effect would come - that reduction in NAV discount - from a rising stock price. We might even think that some of both happens.

But we, of course, are not less informed investors. So we can look up how much of that distribution is return of capital:

For the first 6 months of the year 45% of the (OK, 43.89) distribution is return of capital. That gives us a 4.77% "real distribution". That's what our money would be earning in the fund. To be able to compare this with other options that's the right - or very close to being the right - number to be using. And that's actually pretty good. For non-taxpayers it's still - a little - above what we can gain pretty much risk free but taxable in USFR. Or, again a little, over what we can get with less interest rate risk in SHYM but again non-taxable.

For higher rate taxpayers it becomes very attractive indeed.

Why I'm wrong

My biggest worry here would be that single state muni funds are diversified, yes, across different issuers. But they're all, rather and sort of, linked issuers. If the state finances themselves start to fail then we'd not assume that all of the instate borrowers would be unaffected. So I think we're not wholly diversified even though we're in a couple of hundred issues. But that's something to worry about in extremis, not now.

My opinion

I generally dislike the use of return of capital to boost distributions. I'm not - as above - wholly sure that the usual story wholly works, that this reduces net asset value down to market. I think there can be some buying which boosts market price - which, obviously, disappears when the discount being addressed does.

But my bigger worry is that it can mislead. If people don't realise that near half of what they think is their income is, in fact, their own capital coming back then they can misallocate their spending decisions. They need to be putting a substantial portion - like, about that near half - of their supposed income back into investments in order to maintain their capital for the long term.

But as long as that's understood I think it's fine. For, you know, us informed sophisticates.

The thing is that even after adjusting for RoC the return is still substantial. So, NQP is a good buy for a certain very specific set of investors. Higher income tax rate paying investors in Pennsylvania, those looking for income from their investments, could and should do well here. Out of state, obviously, it's of no great use. So, if you meet the investor profile then this could be a good buy.

data.title: Fed Narrows 11th Consecutive Quarterly Loss Due To Quantitative Tightening
data.description:
Last Friday, when all eyes from the financial markets were focused on Fed Chair Powell’s remarks at the Kansas City Fed’s Jackson Hole Economic Symposium, the Fed quietly released their operating results for the quarter ending June 30, 2025.

While most Fed Watchers tried to parse Chair Powell’s comments about employment, inflation, and how the Fed might respond to the conflict in direction between the two key economic indicators of their dual mandate, not one word was uttered about the Fed’s precarious financial position.

What the quarterly results showed, however, was that the Fed lost money on an operating basis for the 11th consecutive quarter. These are realized losses.

Further, no attention was given to the almost $1 trillion unrealized loss the Fed is carrying on their balance sheet.

Why The Fed Is Losing Money

The main reason the Fed has been losing money is that they have an asset/liability mismatch.

Simply put, this means that the Fed owns long-term fixed rate securities (Treasury Bonds and Mortgage-Backed Securities) and funds them with short-term variable rate liabilities (Bank Reserves and Reverse Repurchases Agreements.)

Creating an asset/liability mismatch is a fundamental error in bank management, as it exposes the institution to a rise in interest rates. When interest rates go up, the cost of the bank’s liabilities increases, while the market value of their assets decline.

The Fed started losing money in 4Q22 when the interest expense they were paying on their short-term liabilities rose above the income they were earning on their fixed rate assets, due to the Fed tightening to fight inflation. They have been losing money ever since.

Quarterly Operating Results

The Fed lost $6.2 billion from operations in the second quarter, a significant improvement from last year’s second quarter operating loss of $16.9 billion.

This was their smallest quarterly deficit since they last made a profit in 3Q22.

While Interest Income fell by -11.4% to $39.6 billion y/y, Interest Expense plunged by -23.7% to $45.1 billion y/y.

There are three factors that have caused the Fed’s quarterly operating results to gradually improve:

  1. The Fed has reduced their asset/liability mismatch through Quantitative Tightening (QT).
  2. The cost of the Fed’s liabilities is 100 basis points lower than a year ago.
  3. The average yield on the Fed’s Treasury holdings has increased by 17 basis points y/y.

The Fed Has Reduced Their Asset / Liability Mismatch Through QT

The mismatch came into being when the Fed began their Quantitative Easing (QE) Program during the Great Financial Crisis of 2008 to inject liquidity into the system. The Fed deviated from their historical practice of buying short maturity Treasury Securities as they purchased longer maturity Treasury Notes and Bonds, and Mortgage-Backed Securities. The Fed’s balance Sheet expanded tenfold during QE, rising from less than $900 billion in total assets to almost $9 trillion at the peak.

The liquidity injection was always intended to be temporary, and when the Fed began to fight the rise in inflation, they implemented their Policy Normalization Plan. Part of the plan was to reduce the size of their balance sheet, more commonly referred to as QT, thereby draining the liquidity (Bank Reserves and Reverse Repurchase Agreements) that was causing the inflation.

As the Fed’s assets increased through QE, so too did their liquidity. Pre-QE, the liquidity on the Fed balance sheet was $54 billion, and it grew more than 100-fold to a high of $6.1 trillion.

Since QT began, the Fed has reduced both their total assets and their liquidity by $2.3 trillion from their peak. This has caused the asset/liability mismatch to narrow, for in percentage terms, the liquidity has declined by 38% while total assets have shrunk by only 26%.

Total assets are currently $6.6 trillion, while liquidity stands at $3.7 trillion. Total assets are at their lowest level since 2Q20, while liquidity is at its lowest level since 1Q21.

The Cost Of The Fed’s Liabilities Is 100 Basis Points Lower Than A Year Ago

The Fed cut the Fed Funds rate by 100 basis points during the final third of 2024 to the range of 4.25-4.50%. The cost of the Fed’s variable rate liabilities is closely tied to Fed Funds, so last year, the Fed paid an average of 5.33% on their liabilities, while this year they paid only 4.33%. This resulted in substantial savings in interest expense to the Fed and helped narrow the Fed’s Negative Net Interest Margin.

The market’s preoccupation with Chair Powell’s comments at Jackson Hole was to try to glean when the Fed will next cut rates. Based on his statement “with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance,” the market is looking for another rate cut in September.

CME FedWatch is projecting a 90% probability of a 25 basis point cut.

The Average Yield On The Fed’s Treasury Securities Has Been Rising

When the Fed began QT, they were allowing $60 billion in maturing Treasury Securities to runoff each month. As the Fed’s balance sheet has shrunk, they have twice tapered their monthly runoff. First, on June 1, 2024, the monthly Treasury runoff was cut to $25 billion per month, then, effective April 1, 2025, the monthly Treasury runoff was tapered again to only $5 billion per month. The Fed tapered their Treasury runoffs to slow down the process as they approached their desired level of Ample Reserves, to prevent the type of liquidity crisis that popped up in September 2019, when QT was shrinking too abruptly.

As I mentioned in my previous Seeking Alpha article, “Fed to Begin Tapering Their Balance Sheet Runoff And Other Observations”:

The Fed has been clear in why they are tapering their QT policy normalization program. The last time QT was attempted, in 2017-2019, the Fed went too fast and they experienced a liquidity problem. Fed Funds spiked from 2% to almost 10%. This caused them to immediately stop QT and reverse policy to stem the liquidity imbalance.

The Fed learned their lesson from that experience, so as a result, they want to move more slowly to prevent a recurrence. Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan, at a recent Monetary Policy Conference, stated "slowing…doesn’t mean stopping. In fact…proceeding more gradually may allow the Fed to eventually get to a smaller balance sheet."

In doing so, the Fed began reinvesting the proceeds of Treasury Securities that were maturing above the new monthly cap. These mostly were securities that were purchased when yields were around 2.00% and were now being reinvested in Treasury Securities yielding the current market rate of around 4.00%. The net impact of the reinvestment of maturing Treasuries at a higher yield was to raise the overall yield of the Fed’s Treasury position. At June 30, 2024, the Fed’s Treasury holdings were yielding 2.22%, and at June 30, 2025, the Fed’s smaller Treasury position, due to QT, was now yielding 2.39%.

How The Fed’s Average Weekly Operating Loss Has Improved

Progress in the Fed’s weekly operating loss can be seen in the following chart. As mentioned above, the Fed’s shrinking asset/liability mismatch and narrower Negative Net Interest Margin have resulted in an improvement in the Fed’s operating results.

The following chart shows the 4-week average weekly loss to smooth out technical distortions. During last year’s second quarter, the Fed was losing roughly -$1.3 billion per week, while for 2Q25 the Fed’s average weekly was approximately -$600 million per week.

Cumulative Operating Losses Are Carried As A Deferred Asset On the Fed’s Balance Sheet

The Fed’s cumulative loss from operations over the 11 quarters is -$234 billion. This is more than five times the Fed’s total capital of $45.5 billion.

Fortunately, the Fed uses a lenient accounting system that allows them to carry the loss on their balance sheet as a deferred asset, so their capital account is not impaired.

For a detailed analysis please see my Seeking Alpha article, “How The Fed Turns A $105 Billion Loss Into An Asset.”

Unrealized SOMA Portfolio Losses

The Fed is also carrying another loss on its Balance Sheet.

The market value of the Fed’s bond holdings in its SOMA portfolio is significantly below the cost of purchasing those securities. This is because the bulk of the securities were purchased during the Fed’s QE period when interest rates were much lower than they are now.

As can be seen in the chart below, since QT began, the yield on the 10-year Treasury Note has more than doubled to 4.26%.

As a result, the market value of the Fed’s Treasury Securities and their Mortgage-Backed Securities is $927 billion below their cost. The Fed uses another lenient accounting treatment that allows them to carry their bond holdings at Amortized Cost. Consequently, this loss in value of their assets only shows up in a footnote on their balance sheet.

The market value of the SOMA portfolio represents a -14% loss from the cost of purchasing the securities.

Fed Policy Going Forward

Fed Governor Christopher Waller, who is a candidate to replace Jay Powell when his term as Fed Chair expires in May 2026, gave a very candid assessment of the Fed’s balance sheet last month in a speech before the Dallas Fed.

For the first time, a senior Fed official publicly acknowledged the balance sheet maturity mismatch and the losses it has caused. He also was specific about when the Fed might end QT.

QT has been a Fed policy for three and one-half years, and many are trying to predict when it will end. Chair Powell has been quite vague about this, stating in his recent Semiannual Congressional Testimony “We’re trying to stop a little bit above what we consider ample, and we think we’re meaningfully above that now…we can’t put a number on it because you can’t directly know the demand for reserves other than by observing behavior in the market and then putting a little bit of a buffer on it.”

Waller was more definitive. He stated he feels the Fed will continue with QT until Bank Reserves fall to $2.7 trillion. Currently they are $3.3 trillion. He arrives at this figure by comparing today’s situation to the last time the Fed implemented QT in 2019. The trouble then began when reserves fell below 8% of GDP, so he picked 9% of GDP as a buffer. GDP is roughly $30 trillion.

Governor Waller went on further, saying that he believes that the Fed will settle with a balance sheet of assets totaling $5.8 trillion.

He gets to this figure by adding the three main liabilities of the Fed: Currency, which historically has been the Fed’s largest liability, is $2.3 trillion. The Treasury General Account, which is the Treasury’s checking account at the Fed, typically averages $780 billion. And Bank Reserves, as mentioned above, will settle at $2.7 trillion. This totals $5.8 trillion.

With current assets at $6.6 trillion and a monthly runoff rate of $20 billion ($5 billion in Treasuries and $15 billion in MBS), Waller’s projection implies QT will continue for another 40 months. This is a much longer period of time than many were projecting.

Conclusion

When the Fed implemented QE to battle the Great Financial Crisis of 2008, they deviated from their traditional policy and created an asset/liability mismatch as they expanded their balance sheet to add liquidity to the system.

Since 2022, they have been trying to Normalize Policy by executing QT as they battle the inflation that appeared due to that liquidity injection.

The cost to the Fed has been huge as they’ve tightened monetary policy.

The Fed has incurred a cumulative operating loss of -$234 billion, which they carry as a deferred asset on their balance sheet, and they also carry an unrealized loss of -$927 billion in their SOMA portfolio.

The Fed has made headway, as their quarterly losses have declined due to the asset/liability mismatch shrinking through QT, hence their desire for it to continue.

The Fed insists that their operating losses and unrealized SOMA losses will not affect their ability to conduct monetary policy.

While this may be true in the short run, the Fed’s financial impairment has contributed to a huge political battle with the current administration that is threatening the Fed’s independence.

The Fed has never been under so much political pressure.

Time will tell if they are able to maintain their independence.

data.title: Updating Gold Ratios To Cyclical Markets
data.description:
Important gold ratios to markets that are more cyclical

When gold rises again vs. these markets the counter-cyclical economic view will generally be back on. Gold has far more utility (insurance, risk-off liquidity, relative value retention) during economically troubled times than cyclical assets like stocks and commodities.

Gold/SPX (U.S. stock market)

A sweet consolidation as the market angst of the spring has gone bye-bye. This is a lovely consolidation that was much needed as gold got over-pumped to the upside (and stocks to the downside) in April.

Gold/Global (World, ex-U.S.)

Another constructive downward consolidation in the monetary metal vs. global stock markets. Sure, it’s below the moving averages, which are about to Death Cross. You’d do well to tune such noise out. It’ll probably spring a rally in the ratio and in my opinion, end the correction.

Gold/CRB Commodity Index

A nice, flat consolidation riding the uptrending 50 day moving average. Counter-cyclical gold laying in wait to out-perform cyclical commodities into the next counter-cycle.

Gold/WTI Crude Oil

Another uptrending counter-cyclical marker, which is another way of saying another indicator trending toward a rude interruption, at least, of this phase of the economic cycle. The gold mining industry would benefit, fundamentally (product up in relation to cost input).

Gold/Copper

The market got a lot of noise injected by Trump expectations vs. what the Trump jawbone actually said (about copper related tariffs). But the moving averages show uptrends in Au/Cu the whole while. Star counter-cyclical metal bullish vs. star cyclical metal.

Gold/Silver Ratio

Moving on to a couple more complicated items, the post-April pullback in the GSR has given life to many “SGR trades” as I call them. Those with an implied tailwind with a rising Silver/Gold ratio. The GSR is still trapped below the moving averages and so, those trades drag on. But at such time as GSR may start to bull and drag the US dollar along with it, best to consider backing away from the punch bowl and stay away from the pool, because it’ll have a strange brown object floating in there.

Gold/Inflation Expectations

This ratio is sliding along bullishly. The indication is that inflation has not been a major problem since 2023. The indication is that the opposite of inflation could even come to be feared before the next inflationary phase.

data.title: RTX Corporation: You Should Not Ignore This Aerospace And Defense Powerhouse
data.description:
RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX), a major player in the aerospace and defense sector, has outperformed the market with a 10.6% stock gain compared to the S&P 500’s 3.8% increase since my previous report. The company released its Q2 2025 earnings in July. I now review the results and provide an updated price target for investors.

RTX Posts Robust Sales Growth

Second quarter sales rose 9% to $21.58 billion, exceeding analysts's expectations by $950 million. The growth was driven by commercial aftermarket sales and good growth in commercial OEM sales despite lower volumes on the Boeing 737 MAX program. Defense sales also showed good growth with a 6% jump. Free cash flow generation was mute, driven by a work stoppage at Pratt & Whitney, but this will be recovered in the balance of the year. Other pressures on cash flow came from a $250 million compensation rendered related to the GTF issues and $125 million in tariff headwinds. Adjusted EPS grew 11% to $1.56, exceeding estimates by $0.13.

Collins Aerospace Grows Aftermarket And Defense Sales

Colins Aerospace sales grew 9% to $7.6 billion with strong growth in commercial aftermarket sales and defense. The aftermarket sales continue to be driven by high asset utilization and limited supply of new airplanes. The limited supply of new airplanes also resulted in Commercial OEM sales growing only 1%, with lower 737 MAX shipset volumes being more than offset by improvements in Boeing 787 volumes. Defense sales were up 11%, driven by the F-35 and the Survivable Airborne Operations Center program. Profits increased 9% to $1.25 billion in adjusted basis, showing stable margins balancing lower R&D, better commercial aftermarket and defense volumes, a strong defense mix with tariff impacts, and an unfavorable commercial OEM mix.

Pratt & Whitney Grows Double Digits

Pratt & Whitney reports sales growth of 12% to $7.63 billion, driven by 19% growth in commercial aftermarket volumes. While Collins saw flat OEM sales, Pratt saw its commercial OEM sales grow by 15%, driven by volume and mix. The defense revenues also contrasted with Collin’s performance, posting flat revenues driven by flat F-35 engine volumes and contract award timing. Adjusted operating profits grew 13%, offsetting the impact of tariffs and the strike at Pratt as well as an unfavorable aftermarket mix.

Raytheon Shows Positive Results From Productivity Transformation

Raytheon reported sales growth of 6% to $7 billion, driven by higher volumes, and we saw a strong improvement in margins from 10.8% to 11.6%, driving operating profits up to $809 million. At times, the Raytheon business lagged from a performance perspective, but the company has implemented productivity measures through a digital backbone that is paying off.

RTX Q2 Earnings Show Tariffs Offsetting Production Efficiency and Lower R&D

What is unfortunate for RTX is that its progress in driving margins up in Raytheon through implementing a digital core and benefiting from lower R&D and good volumes in its other segments is being offset by tariffs. The company has absorbed $125 million in tariff hits out of a revised figure of $500 million. I believe that RTX’s earnings show which parties are suffering from the tariffs, and those are mostly customers, U.S. corporations, and shareholders.

RTX Sees Higher Sales Growth, EPS Revised Down On Tariff Impact

Despite tariff challenges, there has been a positive revision to the full-year outlook. The company has revised its sales growth from 4%-6% to 6%-7%. This is driven by Collins showing high single-digit sales instead of mid-single digits, Pratt moved up from high single-digit growth to low double-digit, and the outlook for Raytheon remained stable at mid-single digit growth. Adjusted EPS has been revised down by $0.20 to $5.80-$5.95, driven by a $0.30 tariff headwind partially offset by tax benefits. Free cash flow guidance remained stable at $7-$7.5 billion. Tariffs are now expected to have a $500 million negative impact net of offsets, an improvement compared to the $850 million expected earlier.

RTX is leveraging the USMCA agreement and making existing parts USMCA compliant while also leveraging military duty-free exemptions.

RTX Stock Is Undervalued Against Aerospace Peers

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

In terms of EBITDA, estimates for 2025 have barely changed. In 2026, there is <1% revision upward and 1.7% upward for 2027. This drives the CAGR from 3.4% to 3.9% with a total EBITDA generation increase of 0.8% between 2025 and 2027. On free cash flow, estimates have improved, driven by lower expected impact of tariffs this year. This drives a 3.3% higher free cash flow than initially expected this year. For 2026, there is a small increase of less than 1% and a 3.3% increase in 2027. This improved the CAGR to 27.3%, and total free cash flow generation has been revised up by 2.4%.

Based on FY26 earnings and a valuation between the company median and the peer group, I am raising my price target for RTX to $190.60, implying 19% upside.

Conclusion: RTX Corporation Stock Remains Attractive

Second quarter earnings showed pressure from tariffs. Absent of tariffs, we would have likely seen margin expansion as the company is growing its volumes. Even with tariff impacts, I expect that the estimated increases in airplane production, most notably the Boeing 737 MAX, will positively drive earnings going forward, while we also note that demand for aftermarket services, missiles, and missile defense systems remains robust. As a result, I believe the stock remains compelling for investors looking to benefit from sales momentum in the commercial airplane and defense industries.

data.title: Atour Lifestyle Q2: The Growth Outlook Accelerates
data.description:
Atour Lifestyle Holdings Limited (NASDAQ:ATAT) reported the company’s Q2 results on the 26th of August. The Chinese hotel chain franchiser continues to expand at an accelerating as the company's another 2025 growth guidance raise suggests. At the same time, marketing investments continue to drive brand recognition for Atour’s retail business.

I remained at a Buy rating in my previous April article on the stock, titled “Atour Lifestyle: The Growth Story Remains Undervalued”. The stock has since returned 58%, well above the S&P 500’s return of 21%.

Atour Q2: The Growth Outlook Accelerates

Atour continued rapid growth in Q2. Revenues grew by 37.4% year-on-year to RMB 2.47 billion ($345 million), beating Wall Street’s consensus by $19 million through accelerated momentum. The quarter’s adjusted EPS of $0.42 beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.04.

The main hotel revenue stream has continued to grow; Atour’s franchise expansion remains on track. Manachised hotel revenues reached RMB 1.30 billion, up by 26.5% year-on-year as Atour’s hotel footprint expanded to a total of 1824 hotels. The number of hotels is up by 29.2% year-on-year. Most importantly, the growth pipeline remains incredibly good with 816 additional manachised hotels under development at the end of Q2.

Underlying operational metrics were fair in the hotel segment. The average daily room rate “ARD” saw pressure that again slightly weakened growth, this time being down by -1.8% to RMB 433. The pace of ADR pressure has started to moderate from a -5.8% decline in 2024 and a -2.8% decline in Q1, though – travel agency operator Trip.com (TCOM) also noted stabilizing industry-wide hotel room rates in China in the May earnings call, and it seems that such a notation is holding true. I believe that gradually stabilizing ADR is very positive for Atour; while not very critical to Atour’s immediate topline growth level, stabilizing rates should encourage franchisees to invest more in new hotels as the earnings outlook improves. With such a dynamic, a stabilizing ADR could contribute well towards Atour's long-term growth.

More negatively, hotel occupancy did decline by 200 basis points year-on-year to 76.4%. The decline reflects increasing competition and a volatile macroeconomic backdrop, not being detrimental to Atour’s longer-term outlook yet. Increasing competition seems to continue weighing on ADR and occupancy, but Atour has overall continued to maintain a healthy mix in spite of an increasingly challenging competitive landscape.

Retail revenues continue to drive even faster topline growth. Atour’s brands reached RMB 965 million in retail revenues for Q2 alone, up by 79.8% year-on-year. Atour’s brand recognition continues to grow and the company continues to innovate its product lineup to drive further growth. Notably, retail revenues did decline sequentially, but Atour has also previously experienced quarterly fluctuations in the retail segment revenue performance. A growing hotel footprint works as a growing platform for brand recognition over the long term, likely to drive further momentum to the retail business in upcoming quarters and years.

Profitability came in at a fairly good level. Adjusted EBITDA grew by 37.7% year-on-year to RMB 610 million, reflecting rapid topline growth and increased gross margins. The hotel gross margin increased by 260 basis points year-on-year to 38.3%, and the retail segment gross margin increased by 270 basis points to 53.3% through increased scale. Operating expense growth did offset a good part of margin gains, though - Atour increased marketing expenses massively to RMB 393 million, up by 74.9% year-on-year to drive retail segment growth. As long as the retail segment continues to grow, I don’t believe such marketing investments to be cause for concern.

Atour’s outlook remains very good. The company now guides 30% revenue growth for 2025, up from the previous 25-30% guidance range. As I wrote in my recent article on Trip.com, China is opening up the country for tourism with visa-free policies and social media visibility. Inbound tourism has continued to surge, in turn boosting the hospitality industry’s outlook while Atour continues to expand its nationwide presence. Investments in retail segment marketing should also contribute well to future growth.

Atour’s Valuation Has Upside Left

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Atour. I have raised my revenue expectations to a 2024-2034 CAGR of 13.4% with 3% perpetual growth after the estimate period. I still assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 25%, and that Atour converts after-tax earnings well into free cash flow through the capital light manachised hotel operating model.

The estimates put Atour’s fair value at $51.1, 39% above the stock price at the time of writing – as Atour’s growth has even accelerated, the stock still undervalues Atour’s long-term earnings potential. I believe that tailwinds continue to make the stock an attractive investment at the current price.

The fair value estimate is up from $43.1 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.13%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t expect Atour to draw a noteworthy amount of debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.27% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.25% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for China. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.04. With a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.13%.

Takeaway

Atour’s Q2 report was good as revenue growth accelerated. The hotel expansion pipeline remains healthy even though the ADR and occupancy have continued to see some pressure, and earnings continue to benefit from scale. Retail momentum remains very healthy as Atour has invested massively in increased marketing. The stock still undervalues long-term earnings potential, and I remain with a Buy rating for ATAT stock.
